tossups.question: In an 1896 novel, this author’s guests realize that “poetry and beauty” have died with this author, who tells his boss to “commit murder, but write no verses” in a letter he reads at a party just before his death. In a book by this author, overzealous firemen allow the protagonist to escape from a party staffed by perpetually singing servants. In a novel, the slave Eunice commits suicide alongside this author, who intercedes to have a hostage placed under his nephew (*) Marcus’s care. This author’s time as “Arbiter of Elegance” was fictionalized in Henryk Sienkiewicz’s (“shen-KEH-vitch’s”) Quo Vadis. Quartilla forces Giton and Encolpius to participate in an orgy in a novel by this author, who used a lavish feast given by Trimalchio to satirize Roman society. For 10 points, name this author of the Satyricon.
tossups.question_sanitized: In an 1896 novel, this author's guests realize that "poetry and beauty" have died with this author, who tells his boss to "commit murder, but write no verses" in a letter he reads at a party just before his death. In a book by this author, overzealous firemen allow the protagonist to escape from a party staffed by perpetually singing servants. In a novel, the slave Eunice commits suicide alongside this author, who intercedes to have a hostage placed under his nephew (*) Marcus's care. This author's time as "Arbiter of Elegance" was fictionalized in Henryk Sienkiewicz's ("shen-KEH-vitch's") Quo Vadis. Quartilla forces Giton and Encolpius to participate in an orgy in a novel by this author, who used a lavish feast given by Trimalchio to satirize Roman society. For 10 points, name this author of the Satyricon.

bonuses.leadin: In this novel, the poet Eumolpus (“yoo-MOHL-pus”) narrates a “Milesian (“my-LEEZH-in”) tale” about seducing his employer’s son as a tutor in Pergamum. For 10 points each:
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: In this novel, the poet Eumolpus ("yoo-MOHL-pus") narrates a "Milesian ("my-LEEZH-in") tale" about seducing his employer's son as a tutor in Pergamum. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this novel, in which stories about witches and a werewolf are told at a banquet held by a wealthy freedman.
In another embedded narrative from the Satyricon, this woman offers her husband’s corpse to replace one that was stolen while her lover was on guard duty.

In another story from the Satyricon, Trimalchio (“trim-ALL-kee-oh”) recounts meeting the Cumaean (“koo-MEE-in”) Sibyl, one of many of the novel’s allusions to the sixth book of this poet’s Aeneid (“ih-NEE-id”).
parts_sanitized: Name this novel, in which stories about witches and a werewolf are told at a banquet held by a wealthy freedman.
In another embedded narrative from the Satyricon, this woman offers her husband's corpse to replace one that was stolen while her lover was on guard duty.

In another story from the Satyricon, Trimalchio ("trim-ALL-kee-oh") recounts meeting the Cumaean ("koo-MEE-in") Sibyl, one of many of the novel's allusions to the sixth book of this poet's Aeneid ("ih-NEE-id").
answers: the widow of Ephesus [or the matron of Ephesus; or matrona Ephesi]
answers_sanitized: the widow of Ephesus [or the matron of Ephesus; or matrona Ephesi]

bonuses.leadin: After being tricked into drinking a sleep potion instead of poison, a woman from this city wakes up inside a tomb and decides to starve to death, but is freed by robbers who plunder the tomb’s silver and gold. For 10 points each:
parts: Identity this home city of Anthia and Habrocomes, who remain devoted to each other despite being separated and captured numerous times in a 2nd century AD Greek novel by a writer from here named Xenophon.

The Ephesian Tale inspired an extremely similar Latin romance about a man from this city named Apollonius, which John Gower summarizes as an example of incest in his Confessio Amantis. Gower himself narrates a play in which a character from this city wins a tournament wearing rusty armor that had just washed up on shore.

A widow from Ephesus sleeps with a soldier who finds her faithfully standing guard over her husband’s corpse, in a story told in this other ancient novel best-known for its description of the Feast of Trimalchio.
answers: Tyre (the romance and the play are, respectively, Apollonius of Tyre and Pericles, Prince of Tyre)
answers_sanitized: Tyre (the romance and the play are, respectively, Apollonius of Tyre and Pericles, Prince of Tyre)

bonuses.leadin: Name these things related to the comic novel Antic Hay, for 10 points each:
parts: Antic Hay was written by this English author of Crome Yellow as well as Brave New World.
Antic Hay features what may be the first mistaken description of a vomitorium as a place to vomit in an allusion to the Satyricon, a novel by this Roman author.
The title of Antic Hay comes from Edward II, a play by this author who also wrote about a conqueror who dies after burning a Qu’ran in another work.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: Name these things related to the comic novel Antic Hay, for 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Antic Hay was written by this English author of Crome Yellow as well as Brave New World.
Antic Hay features what may be the first mistaken description of a vomitorium as a place to vomit in an allusion to the Satyricon, a novel by this Roman author.
The title of Antic Hay comes from Edward II, a play by this author who also wrote about a conqueror who dies after burning a Qu'ran in another work.

bonuses.leadin: If there was one literary genre the Romans didn’t take from the Greeks, it was satire. For 10 points each:
parts: This poet’s famous phrase quis custodiet ipsos custodies in context mocks the decline of womanly virtue, rather than government institutions. His 3rd Satire is all about the horrors of the foreigners packing Rome.
This poet’s witty epigrams contain such noble advice as Ride, si sapis. He is also a notable literary source of Roman vulgarity, though he insisted “My poems are naughty, but my life is pure”.
This author’s only known work is the first Roman novel that we have, the Satyricon, known for the Cena Trimalchionis. He may have been the fashion adviser of Nero.
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: If there was one literary genre the Romans didn't take from the Greeks, it was satire. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: This poet's famous phrase quis custodiet ipsos custodies in context mocks the decline of womanly virtue, rather than government institutions. His 3rd Satire is all about the horrors of the foreigners packing Rome.
This poet's witty epigrams contain such noble advice as Ride, si sapis. He is also a notable literary source of Roman vulgarity, though he insisted "My poems are naughty, but my life is pure".
This author's only known work is the first Roman novel that we have, the Satyricon, known for the Cena Trimalchionis. He may have been the fashion adviser of Nero.

bonuses.leadin: In this book, Encolpius, Giton, and Eumolpus survive a shipwreck, after which Eumolpus proposes that he take the guise of an childless old man with massive amounts of wealth. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this book in which Encolpius’s killing of a sacred goose does little to cure his impotence. His ailment is eventually lessened after Encolpius sleeps with the daughter of Philomela.
The Satryicon was written by this author, who, according to Tacitus, committed suicide by opening his veins and instructing those around him to recite poetry.
The best known scene from the Satyricon is at one of these events, hosted by Trimalchio. During one of these events from Oliver Twist, the protagonist asks “Please, sir, can I have some more.”
answers: dinners [or feasts; or meals; accept pretty much anything to do with people coming together to eat]
answers_sanitized: dinners [or feasts; or meals; accept pretty much anything to do with people coming together to eat]
bonuses.leadin_sanitized: In this book, Encolpius, Giton, and Eumolpus survive a shipwreck, after which Eumolpus proposes that he take the guise of an childless old man with massive amounts of wealth. For 10 points each:
parts_sanitized: Name this book in which Encolpius's killing of a sacred goose does little to cure his impotence. His ailment is eventually lessened after Encolpius sleeps with the daughter of Philomela.
The Satryicon was written by this author, who, according to Tacitus, committed suicide by opening his veins and instructing those around him to recite poetry.
The best known scene from the Satyricon is at one of these events, hosted by Trimalchio. During one of these events from Oliver Twist, the protagonist asks "Please, sir, can I have some more."

bonuses.leadin: One character in this work owns a mural showing a dog with a chain on its neck that is accompanied with a sign that warns visitors to beware of the dog. For 10 points each:
parts: Name this book in which Encolpius seeks the help of Oenothea to cure his impotence in a ritual that occurs after he accidentally kills a goose. Perhaps its most notable portion contains an episode wherein Encolpius is shocked to eat a fertilized egg with a chicken inside of it.
This man is generally held to be the creator of Trimalchio and the author of the Satyricon.

This character from the Satyricon is seduced by a soldier while mourning the loss of her husband, eventually agreeing to let her husband’s body take the place of a condemned man’s corpse so that the soldier is not punished for failing in his guard duties.
answers: The Widow of Ephesus [prompt on partial answer]
answers_sanitized: The Widow of Ephesus [prompt on partial answer]
